description: The provided image is a publisher placeholder icon labeled 'Check for updates' and does not contain any scientific data, micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectra, or mechanical curves. Consequently, the specific multiphase microstructural features, XRD peak positions, EBSD phase distributions, Kernel Average Misorientation maps, TEM substructures, EPMA line profiles, or stress–strain behavior of the Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T) cannot be visually extracted or verified. The caption is also absent, preventing identification of whether the intended figure should depict microscopy, diffraction, mechanical testing, or compositional analysis. All reported characterization details—including phase volume fractions, grain sizes, carbide distributions, retained austenite stability, and deformation-induced martensite kinetics—remain unverifiable from this unreadable figure, which appears to be a software interface element rather than an experimental result.

The figure presents a two-part overview of the thermomechanical processing route and sample preparation for Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel. The upper panel displays the complete heat-treatment protocol: homogenization at 1200 °C for 60 min, hot rolling from 1100 °C to 880 °C, air cooling, 60 % cold rolling, intercritical annealing at 750, 800, or 850 °C for 30 min between the Ac1 and Ac3 temperatures, and low-bainite aging at 400 °C for 30 min. Inset micrographs show the microstructural evolution from the hot-rolled state, through the heavily deformed cold-rolled lamellar structure, to the intercritically annealed morphology and the final multiphase structure after tempering. The lower panel shows a photograph of the thin-wall low-density steel sheet with the rolling direction (RD) indicated, together with the dog-bone tensile specimen geometry machined from the 1.6 mm plate, featuring a gauge length of 14 mm, width of 5 mm, thickness of 1 mm, and overall length of 58 mm. The figure demonstrates that industrial-scale thermomechanical processing produces thin-gauge multiphase low-density steel suitable for standardized uniaxial tensile testing.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images showing multiphase microstructure evolution in Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel during rolling and subsequent heat treatment. Subfigures (a) and (b) display the hot-rolled and cold-rolled states, respectively, revealing a banded microstructure primarily composed of bainite (B) and fresh martensite (FM). Subfigures (c–e) show intercritically annealed samples at 750 °C (AN750), 800 °C (AN800), and 850 °C (AN850), where ferrite (F), FM, B, and retained austenite (RA) are visible, with RA content decreasing as annealing temperature increases. Subfigures (f–h) present the corresponding tempered conditions (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T) after low bainite aging at 400 °C, where tempered martensite (TM) appears alongside F, B, FM, and RA. The images demonstrate that increasing intercritical annealing temperature promotes ferrite growth and reduces RA volume fraction, while subsequent tempering transforms part of the microstructure to TM. These microstructural variations support the development of heterogeneous multiphase architectures for optimizing strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents the evolution of retained austenite (RA) during thermomechanical processing of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel. Panels (a)–(d) are EBSD phase maps showing the spatial distribution of BCC (red) and FCC (green) phases, alongside tabulated phase fractions and RA particle-size histograms: the hot-rolled HR sample is fully BCC (FCC fraction 0); AN750 (intercritically annealed at 750°C) contains an FCC fraction of 0.030 with an average RA diameter of 0.59 μm; AN800 (800°C) shows an FCC fraction of 0.011 and an average diameter of 0.72 μm; AN850 (850°C) retains only an FCC fraction of 0.002 with an average diameter of 0.83 μm. Panel (e) quantifies RA content in volume percent, peaking at 3.0 vol% for AN750 and decreasing to 1.1 vol% for AN800 and 0.2 vol% for AN850. Panel (f) provides XRD patterns with indexed α (BCC) and γ (FCC) reflections, where γ peak intensities progressively diminish from AN750 to AN850. Collectively, the data demonstrate that increasing intercritical annealing temperature markedly reduces RA volume fraction while marginally increasing its average particle size, with the 750°C treatment yielding the highest RA content and stability.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM-based microstructural characterization of multiphase Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T, processed by intercritical annealing at 750, 800, and 850 °C respectively followed by bainite aging at 400 °C. The montage includes bright-field and dark-field TEM images revealing ferrite (F), bainite (B), tempered martensite (TM), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA) at scales from 2 μm down to 100 nm. Distinctive carbide orientation—approximately 60° to bainitic ferrite boundaries in B versus random distribution in TM—enables differentiation of B from TM, which EBSD cannot resolve. A selected-area electron diffraction pattern indexed as [110]γ confirms the FCC structure of RA. An XRD subplot shows BCC α and FCC γ peaks across the three tempered samples, supporting decreasing retained austenite content with increasing annealing temperature (7.4%, 2.4%, 0.6%). Collectively, these results verify the multiphase heterogeneous architecture and establish TEM criteria for distinguishing B/TM while quantifying RA stability.

This Figure presents an integrated EBSD-based phase separation strategy and interphase strain analysis for low-density Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si steel subjected to three processing conditions: AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T. The top row shows combined Kikuchi pattern quality (IQ) and phase distribution maps, where FCC retained austenite (green) decreases visibly from 0.074 to 0.006 fraction with increasing annealing temperature, while BCC phases (red) dominate; magnified insets resolve fresh martensite (FM), bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), and ferrite (F) via IQ contrast, with marked IQ FM values of 39 532.6, 41 731.4, and 35 468.2, and IQ B/TM values of 64 306.6, 67 252.1, and 72 402.3, respectively. The three histograms display bimodal IQ distributions for the BCC structure across all samples, with Peak I shifting from ~41 750 to ~48 120 and Peak II rising from ~71 477 to ~86 105, reflecting distinct band contrasts between soft ferrite (~5 μm) and hard FM/B/TM (~1 μm). The KAM maps reveal heterogeneous strain partitioning, and the point-to-origin KAM profiles show sharp peaks at phase boundaries due to geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation, with average KAM values of 0.53°, 0.69°, and 0.62° for AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T, respectively. AN800-T exhibits the highest interfacial strain concentration, correlating with its greatest bainite/martensite content. These observations demonstrate that pronounced strain gradients at ferrite/B-TM-FM interfaces coordinate deformation compatibility and underpin the multiphase back-stress strengthening mechanism in this heterogeneous steel.

Figure 6 presents microstructural characteristics and mechanical properties of multiphase low-density steels intercritically annealed at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C followed by 400°C bainite aging (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T). Panels (a) and (b) quantify volume fractions and average phase diameters of ferrite (F), bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA). Increasing annealing temperature from 750°C to 850°C reduces RA from 7.4% to 0.6% and FM from 8.3% to 3.3%, while ferrite coarsens from 4.53 μm to 5.35 μm and B/TM refines to 0.86 μm in AN850-T. Panel (c) shows engineering stress–strain curves: AN750-T displays the lowest ultimate tensile strength (1068 MPa) but superior elongation (34.0%); AN800-T reaches the highest UTS (1295 MPa) with 18.7% elongation; AN850-T achieves 1269 MPa with 13.9% elongation. Panel (d) benchmarks strength–ductility against QP, DP, TRIP, and TBF steels, showing the present steels, particularly AN750-T with a PSE of about 36 GPa%, exceed conventional advanced high-strength steel levels. These data demonstrate that tuning intercritical annealing temperature optimizes heterogeneous multiphase structures for enhanced mechanical performance.

This figure comprises three dual-y-axis line graphs showing the evolution of deformation-induced martensite (DIM) and retained austenite (RA) volume fractions as a function of true strain, modeled with the Olson-Cohen (O-C) model using parameters α = 5, β = 1.28, and n = 0.55. In each panel, the black curve (left axis) tracks the increasing DIM fraction, while the red curve (right axis) tracks the decreasing RA fraction. The three sub-panels correspond to the Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel processed via hot rolling, 60% cold rolling, intercritical annealing at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C for 30 min with air cooling, followed by low bainite aging at 400°C for 30 min, with initial RA levels of approximately 7.4%, 2.4%, and 0.6% respectively, consistent with the AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T samples reported in the text. As strain increases, RA monotonically decreases while DIM increases, confirming the TRIP effect. The final DIM fractions reach about 4.74%, 1.16%, and 0.28% for the three conditions, demonstrating that lower intercritical annealing temperatures preserve more RA, thereby enabling stronger deformation-induced martensitic transformation and enhanced strain-hardening capacity.

The figure presents EPMA elemental mapping and corresponding line-scan quantification for Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T, which were intercritically annealed at 750°C, 800°C, and 850°C respectively, followed by low bainite aging at 400°C. The top row displays two-dimensional distributions of Fe, C, Mn, Al, and Si acquired along the red dashed lines, revealing that carbon is concentrated in bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM), fresh martensite (FM), and retained austenite (RA). The bottom row shows quantitative compositional profiles (Si, Al, Mn, C in at%) as a function of distance across these phases. With increasing annealing temperature, the carbon distribution becomes progressively more uniform, whereas Mn, Al, and Si show no significant spatial variation. These observations support the quantitative phase-composition strategy by demonstrating that carbon partitioning among B/TM, FM, and RA is sensitive to intercritical annealing temperature, providing essential chemical evidence for understanding the multiphase heterogeneous microstructure and its mechanical behavior.

Figure 9 compares the true stress–true strain constitutive behavior of three tempered low-density steel samples—AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T—displaying experimental curves (red solid lines), iso-work model predictions (black dashed lines), and the back-stress contribution σ_B (shaded area between curves). Caption reports that at necking, the maximum σ_B equals 217 MPa for AN750-T, 300 MPa for AN800-T, and 278 MPa for AN850-T. Beneath each mechanical curve, Digital Image Correlation (DIC) strain-field maps illustrate the evolution of local strain during tensile deformation. The AN750-T panel shows relatively uniform strain in the yield stage followed by stress redistribution (marked “Stress relieving”) during uniform plastic deformation, with circles highlighting distributed strain features. The AN800-T panel exhibits two local strain concentration points that propagate and eventually link at approximately 45°, labeled “Second local strain concentration.” The AN850-T panel also displays strain localization and redistribution. Together, the subplots demonstrate that the growing divergence between experimental and modeled flow stress originates from back-stress work hardening associated with geometrically necessary dislocations at soft–hard domain boundaries, while the DIC sequences confirm that coordinated strain partitioning between ferrite, bainite/tempered martensite, and retained austenite effectively prevents premature failure.

This figure presents TEM microstructural analysis and mechanical stress partitioning of multiphase low-density steels (AN750-T, AN800-T, AN850-T). The upper portion shows bright-field TEM images of as-tempered microstructures before deformation, revealing ferrite (F) surrounded by hard domains of bainite/tempered martensite (B/TM) and fresh martensite (FM), together with carbides, geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs), and dislocation pile-ups marked by yellow T-symbols; the images also indicate the average characteristic size of the back-stress influence zone (R, blue arrows) and the average thickness of the heterogeneous layer (Δh, red arrows). A dark-field TEM image of AN850-T highlights dense dislocation regions. High-magnification views show dislocation pile-ups within soft ferrite grains. Additionally, true stress–strain decomposition curves for AN800-T and AN850-T display the evolution of back stress (σ_B) and the load-bearing contributions from F, B/TM, DIM, RA, and FM. As annealing temperature increases, the B/TM load fraction rises. The caption reports back-stress values of 217, 300, and 278 MPa at necking for AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T, respectively. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that GND accumulation at soft/hard domain boundaries produces back-stress work hardening that harmonizes strain partitioning, suppressing premature failure.

The figure presents SEM characterization of crack paths and fracture surface morphologies in multiphase low-density steels subjected to different intercritical annealing temperatures, specifically AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T (annealed at 750 °C, 800 °C, and 850 °C respectively, followed by tempering at 400 °C). Panel (a) shows microcrack initiation and propagation: microvoids (yellow dashed circles) nucleate predominantly at interfaces between soft and hard domains. In AN750-T and AN800-T, crack tips blunt at hard-domain interfaces and generate secondary cracks (yellow arrows), whereas in AN850-T the hard domains are severed (red arrows), indicating increased brittleness. Panel (b) displays the central fracture zone morphology, where AN750-T exhibits deep dimples indicative of ductile fracture, while AN800-T and AN850-T show quasi-cleavage features (red dashed outlines) originating from fresh martensite alongside dimples. Panel (c) provides bimodal dimple size distributions, with average sizes decreasing from 1.54 μm (AN750-T) to 1.31 μm (AN850-T), and peak positions shifting to smaller values. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that higher annealing temperatures promote brittle fracture mechanisms by hard-domain severance and reduced dimple coalescence, undermining the stress-buffering capacity of the multiphase microstructure.

Figure 12 presents post-fracture EBSD characterization of the necking region in Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel samples AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T, processed via intercritical annealing at 750, 800, and 850 °C respectively followed by 400 °C bainitic aging. Panel (a) shows Kikuchi pattern quality overlaid with austenite phase maps (red), where retained austenite fractions decrease from 0.018 to 0.004 and 0.002 with increasing annealing temperature; yellow dashed boxes indicate magnified regions and yellow arrows mark characteristic line scan paths. Panel (b) displays corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps, highlighting hard regions (B/TM, FM) and ferrite (F), with red ellipses marking areas of high local misorientation. Panel (c) provides point-to-point (P-T-P) orientation difference profiles (red) and Kikuchi pattern quality values (black) along the characteristic lines crossing phase boundaries; sharp orientation jumps and pronounced minima in pattern quality occur at interfaces, accompanied by P-T-P_Ave values of 3.50°, 4.48°, and 3.50°. These observations demonstrate significant strain and geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation at soft-hard domain interfaces, confirming stress partitioning and strain-discontinuity accommodation in the multiphase heterogeneous microstructure after tensile deformation.

This figure presents a dilatometry study comprising a thermal cycle schematic and the corresponding linear thermal expansion curve for the Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel. The left schematic specifies the heating protocol: rapid heating at 10 °C/s to 200 °C, followed by slow heating at 0.01 °C/s to 980 °C with a 300 s hold, and subsequent cooling at −30 °C/s. The right plot shows ΔL versus temperature, from which critical phase-transformation temperatures are extracted: Ac1 = 705 °C, Ac3 = 950 °C, Bf = 380 °C, and Ms = 346 °C. During heating, the slope change in the expansion curve identifies the ferrite-to-austenite transformation range, while the cooling segment captures the bainite and martensite start/finish behavior. These measured critical temperatures define the intercritical annealing window (between Ac1 and Ac3) and the low-bainite aging temperature relative to Ms and Bf, providing the essential thermal boundary conditions for designing the multiphase heterogeneous microstructure reported in the study.

This figure presents EBSD-based microstructural characterization of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel after intercritical annealing at 750 °C (AN750-T), 800 °C (AN800-T), or 850 °C (AN850-T) for 30 min followed by low-bainite aging at 400 °C for 30 min. Panel (a) shows inverse pole figure (IPF) orientation maps before parent grain reconstruction, displaying fine multiphase structures with diverse crystal orientations across all three conditions. Panel (b) presents the corresponding parent grain analysis maps, where significantly larger reconstructed prior-austenite grains are observed, indicating successful reconstruction of parent phase morphology. Panel (c) plots equivalent circle diameter distributions versus area weighted score, comparing measured sizes before reconstruction (red curves) and after parent grain analysis (black curves). Quantified average diameters decrease with increasing annealing temperature: 3.41 μm versus 3.29 μm (Δ = 0.12) for AN750-T, 3.09 μm versus 2.94 μm (Δ = 0.15) for AN800-T, and 2.80 μm versus 2.61 μm (Δ = 0.19) for AN850-T. The progressive reduction in average size and increasing Δ values demonstrate that higher intercritical annealing temperatures refine the reconstructed parent grains and deformation-induced martensite (DIM), supporting microstructural engineering of multiphase heterogeneous steels.
